title: Make the Showcase Things
developmental_frame: In the capstone, Build supplies what the day shows and sells. At 9–10 a child can make a small batch of things and a sign, pulling together hand-making (Q1), problem-solving (Q2), and their first digital creation (Q3). Keep the batch small — a few good items plus one digital piece — so 'finished and good' beats 'lots and rushed', the lesson they've heard all year.
authentic_value_meaning: Value here is having real things to show and sell — the maker's contribution to the day. It's every making skill from the year, aimed at one real event: a small collection the child is proud to put on a table in front of guests.

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child produces the actual stuff of their Showcase Day — things people will look at, pick up, and buy. Knowing real guests will see and judge the work raises the bar in a good way, and the pride of a table full of things you made yourself is a powerful finish to the year of building.

artifact: A small set of things for Showcase Day: a few physical items to show or sell, plus at least one digital piece (a sign, poster, or slideshow made on a screen).
success_criteria: The child made a small batch of good-quality physical things (not many rushed ones).
They made at least one digital piece and can explain every part of it.
The display was set up and tested, with one thing fixed after testing.

lessons.title: What to make for the day
lessons.concept: Decide your small batch: a few items to show or sell, plus one digital piece like a sign. Remember the year's rule — a few good things beat a pile of rushed ones. Choose what you can make really well in the time you have.
lessons.real_example: Your batch might be: six good bookmarks to sell, your phone-stand invention to show, and a digital price sign made on screen. Small, doable, and proud-making.
lessons.apply_task: List your small batch: the physical things and the one digital piece you'll make for the day.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Is your batch small enough to make *well* in the time before the day? Quality over quantity, as always.
lessons.feedback_task: Show your batch plan to a parent and check it fits the time in your step ladder.
lessons.teach_back_task: Explain to a sibling why a small batch of good things beats lots of rushed ones.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: You decide what to make from your year's skills — that's yours. Keep it to what you can genuinely do well.
success_criteria: A small, realistic batch (physical + one digital) is planned.

lessons.title: Make the physical things
lessons.concept: Make your batch of physical items using everything you've learned about hand-making — plan, gather, make well, finish. These are going in front of guests, so make each one something you'd be proud to sell.
lessons.real_example: Making six bookmarks: you plan the design, gather materials, and make each one carefully. They don't have to be identical — but each should be good enough that you'd happily hand it over.
lessons.apply_task: Make your batch of physical things, finishing each to a quality you're proud of.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Would you pay for these yourself? If not, which needs improving before the day?
lessons.feedback_task: Show your finished items to an honest family member and ask which is best and which needs work.
lessons.teach_back_task: Show someone your batch and explain how you kept the quality up.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Hand-making is yours — the care in each item is what makes guests want them. No shortcut replaces good making.
success_criteria: A small batch of good-quality physical items is finished.

lessons.title: Make the digital sign
lessons.concept: Make your digital piece — a sign, price list, or slideshow — using your screen-making skill. Keep the golden rule: understand every part. A neat digital sign makes your stall look real and welcoming.
lessons.real_example: A digital sign reading 'Aisha's Handmade Bookmarks — RM2 each' with a little drawing, made on screen and printed out, makes the whole stall look proper.
lessons.apply_task: Make your digital sign or slideshow from a quick plan. Be ready to explain every part of it.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Can you explain every part of your digital piece? If any bit is a mystery, learn it or change it.
lessons.feedback_task: Show your digital piece to a parent and have them check it's clear and readable from a distance.
lessons.teach_back_task: Walk someone through how you made your digital sign, part by part.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: If AI helped with an image or layout, you must still understand and have chosen it — **never show a part you can't explain.** The sign is yours because you understand it.
success_criteria: A digital piece is finished and the child can explain every part.

lessons.title: Set it up and test
lessons.concept: Before the day, set up your display exactly as it'll be — items laid out, sign up. Then test it like a guest would see it, and fix the one thing that looks off. A tested setup means a calm, proud day.
lessons.real_example: You lay out your stall the night before and notice the sign is hard to read from where guests will stand. You move it higher — problem fixed before anyone arrives.
lessons.apply_task: Set up your full display and look at it as a guest would. Fix the one thing that most needs it.
lessons.reflection_prompt: What looked different once it was all set up? Seeing it as a guest often reveals a quick fix.
lessons.feedback_task: Have a family member look at your setup as a 'pretend guest' and say what catches their eye or confuses them.
lessons.teach_back_task: Teach someone why testing the setup beforehand makes the real day go smoothly.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Seeing your real display in your real space is something only you can do — trust your own eyes and a family member's.
success_criteria: The full display was set up and tested from a guest's view.
One improvement was made before the day.
